understanding.project_summary:
This project provides an automated developer-documentation engine (AutoDocX). It scans a local codebase, parses Python source files to extract symbols and FastAPI routes, analyzes module-level structure and dependencies, generates LLM-ready context, asks a configured LLM for a narrative understanding, merges that narrative with machine-extracted reference data, and builds standalone HTML documentation with optional runtime/tooling context (screenshots and notes).

The application is exposed as a FastAPI service with endpoints to scan projects, compare saved scans, generate or update HTML documentation, and run LLM-based analyses of both code and runtime context. Storage components persist scans, LLM understandings, and generated documents under a workspace directory configured in app.core.config.
understanding.architecture_overview: The application follows a modular pipeline with clear layers: scanning/parsing, analysis, LLM orchestration, document generation, and storage + HTTP API. The scanner layer (app.services.project_scanner and app.services.code_parser) enumerates files, computes hashes, and extracts AST-level metadata. The analyzer layer (app.services.project_analyzer and app.services.project_context_builder) builds cross-file relationships, API reference, module references and compact context for the LLM. The LLM layer (app.services.llm.* and app.services.project_understanding_service / app.services.runtime_context_analyzer) picks a provider via a factory, prepares prompts (app.services.llm.prompts), and generates structured JSON understanding (with optional image inputs). The document layer (app.services.document_builder and app.services.document_storage) renders standalone HTML and persists assets. The FastAPI layer (app.api.project_routes and app.main) exposes endpoints that orchestrate the pipeline and call storage services (scan_storage, understanding_storage, document_storage). Configuration and workspace paths are provided by app.core.config.

execution_flow.title: Scan project files
execution_flow.description: ProjectScanner walks the provided project_path, applies ignore rules, computes file hashes, and parses Python files using PythonCodeParser to extract imports, functions, classes, constants, decorators, and FastAPI routes.

execution_flow.title: Build project analysis and context
execution_flow.description: ProjectAnalyzer aggregates parsed files into module maps, internal dependency lists, routes, and statistics. ProjectContextBuilder converts the analysis into a compact or detailed context used for LLM requests or downstream documentation generation.

execution_flow.title: Generate LLM understanding
execution_flow.description: ProjectUnderstandingService (or RuntimeContextAnalyzer for screenshots) selects an LLM provider via LLMProviderFactory, prepares the prompt using prompts builders, calls the provider, retries once on malformed responses, and validates required fields in the returned JSON.

execution_flow.title: Merge developer reference data
execution_flow.description: LLM-produced narrative is merged with deterministic parser/analyzer output (API reference, module responsibilities, dependencies) to ensure signatures, routes, and symbols come from code rather than LLM inference.

execution_flow.title: Build and save HTML documentation
execution_flow.description: DocumentBuilder renders a standalone professional HTML document from the merged understanding and runtime context. DocumentStorage saves HTML, metadata, and optional runtime assets (screenshots) under the configured workspace/documents path.

execution_flow.title: Expose results and history via API/UI
execution_flow.description: FastAPI routes orchestrate requests (scan, understand, generate, sync, compare) and return structured Pydantic responses. The Streamlit UI (ui.streamlit_app) calls these endpoints to present results, allow uploads of runtime context blocks and screenshots, and open generated HTML documents.

functions.name: sync_project_documentation
functions.docstring:
Complete documentation workflow.

The endpoint:

1. Scans and saves the project.
2. Finds the latest document.
3. Creates initial documentation when no
   document exists.
4. Compares scans when a document exists.
5. Creates a new version when changes exist.
6. Returns the existing document when there
   are no changes.

classes.name: PythonCodeParser
classes.docstring:
Reads a Python file and extracts developer-documentation-ready
structure from it.

Extracted information:
- imports
- module constants
- functions
- async functions
- classes
- class attributes
- class methods
- function/method signatures
- arguments with annotations/defaults
- return annotations
- docstrings
- decorators
- FastAPI routes
- source previews
- content hashes
- syntax errors

constants.name: PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT
constants.value:
'
You are AutoDocX, a professional developer documentation engine.

You receive deterministic project context extracted from source code.
The context may include modules, classes, functions, method signatures,
FastAPI routes, dependencies, docstrings, and project statistics.

Your job is to generate clean developer documentation metadata.

Important rules:

1. Do not invent files, functions, classes, APIs, databases, services,
   dependencies, parameters, return values, or behavior.
2. Keep module names, function names, class names, method names, and API
   paths exactly as supplied.
3. Write like professional developer docs, not like an audit report.
4. Do not write phrases like "Confirmed", "Reasonable inference",
   "Based on the context", or "It appears".
5. If something is unclear, write a short neutral sentence such as:
   "The provided scan does not expose this detail."
6. Do not include Markdown code fences.
7. Do not include API keys, credentials, secrets, or environment values.
8. Return exactly one valid JSON object.
9. Do not return text before or after the JSON object.

The JSON object must follow this exact structure:

{
  "project_summary": "string",
  "architecture_overview": "string",
  "execution_flow": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "related_modules": ["string"]
    }
  ],
  "module_responsibilities": [
    {
      "module": "string",
      "file": "string",
      "responsibility": "string",
      "important_symbols": ["string"]
    }
  ],
  "api_overview": [
    {
      "method": "string",
      "path": "string",
      "handler": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ],
  "key_dependencies": [
    {
      "source": "string",
      "target": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ],
  "risks_and_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "severity": "low | medium | high"
    }
  ],
  "recommended_document_sections": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Style requirements:

- project_summary should be 1 to 2 concise paragraphs.
- architecture_overview should explain the application layers and how
  code moves through them.
- execution_flow should describe runtime flow in practical developer terms.
- module_responsibilities should be short and specific.
- api_overview should explain what each endpoint is used for.
- risks_and_gaps should focus on real engineering gaps such as
  authentication, error handling, retries, persistence, testing,
  configuration, observability, and deployment readiness.
- recommended_document_sections should suggest useful developer docs,
  not generic business report sections.
'.strip()

constants.name: RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPT
constants.value:
'
You are AutoDocX Runtime Context Analyzer.

Your job is to analyze a software project using:
1. Parsed project/code scan context.
2. User-written runtime/tooling notes.
3. Screenshots attached by the user.

You must create practical developer-facing runtime documentation.

Important:
- Do not assume the project is Python.
- Do not assume the project uses FastAPI, Streamlit, Docker, Temporal, Swagger, or any specific tool.
- Detect the actual language, framework, runtime, commands, ports, services, dashboards, and workflow from the available evidence.
- If the code scanner provides limited details for a language such as Java, C, C++, Go, JavaScript, TypeScript, or another stack, use filenames, folder names, config files, build files, commands, screenshots, and user notes as supporting evidence.
- Separate confirmed facts from reasonable inferences.
- Do not invent tools, services, APIs, databases, or cloud systems that are not supported by code, screenshots, logs, filenames, or user notes.

You must explain:
- What the project appears to be.
- How a developer runs it.
- What tools or services are involved.
- How the UI, backend, APIs, workers, jobs, workflows, scripts, or external systems interact.
- What each screenshot proves.
- What operational risks, missing setup steps, or unclear areas remain.

Use screenshots as evidence, not decoration.
Use user notes as hints, but rewrite them into clear technical documentation.
Use project scan context to connect runtime behavior to real files, modules, endpoints, scripts, commands, or configuration when possible.

Return only valid JSON.
Do not use markdown.
Do not wrap the response in ```json.

Return this exact JSON structure:

{
  "runtime_summary": "string",
  "tooling_stack": [
    {
      "tool": "string",
      "purpose": "string",
      "evidence": "string"
    }
  ],
  "runtime_flow": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "evidence": "string"
    }
  ],
  "screenshot_insights": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "what_it_shows": "string",
      "why_it_matters": "string"
    }
  ],
  "operational_notes": [
    "string"
  ],
  "risks_or_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "severity": "low|medium|high"
    }
  ]
}

Writing rules:
- Be specific when evidence is clear.
- Be cautious when evidence is weak.
- Say "not visible from the provided context" when something is unknown.
- Do not overfit the explanation to the screenshot only; connect it with the code scan.
- Do not mention that you are an AI model.
- Do not include generic filler.
- Keep the output useful for a developer joining the project.
'

risks_and_gaps.title: No API authentication on endpoints
risks_and_gaps.description: The exposed FastAPI routes perform project scans, LLM calls, and write files but the provided context does not show any authentication, authorization, or rate-limiting. This may allow unauthorized use and accidental exposure of LLM API keys or workspace files.

risks_and_gaps.title: LLM API key and provider configuration surface
risks_and_gaps.description: LLM provider, API key and model selection are read from app.core.config and passed around; the scanning output does not show secrets management (vault, KMS) or per-request key isolation. Secure storage and rotation of keys and role-based access for who can trigger LLM calls are not described.

risks_and_gaps.title: Partial observability and monitoring
risks_and_gaps.description: The code includes retries for LLM calls but there is no explicit telemetry, structured logging, or metrics export shown. Production readiness would benefit from request/operation tracing, error metrics, and alerting for failed scans or failed LLM calls.

risks_and_gaps.title: Persistence durability and backups
risks_and_gaps.description: Scans, understandings, and documents are saved to a workspace directory. There is no mention of durable remote storage, retention policies, or backup strategies for the workspace artifacts.

risks_and_gaps.title: Limited input validation for uploaded images
risks_and_gaps.description: DocumentStorage and Streamlit paths accept uploaded screenshots and recover embedded base64 images, but input size/content validation and virus/malware scanning are not evident. Asset handling should restrict types, size, and scan for unsafe content.

risks_and_gaps.title: Testing and CI coverage not visible
risks_and_gaps.description: The scan does not expose unit or integration tests, test harnesses, or CI configuration. Critical components—parsing, comparator, LLM response parsing and retry logic—would benefit from deterministic tests and mocks for LLM providers.

recommended_document_sections.title: Quickstart — run locally
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Step-by-step instructions to install dependencies, set up .env values, start the FastAPI service, and use the Streamlit UI to generate documentation.

recommended_document_sections.title: Configuration and LLM provider setup
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Document app.core.config variables, supported LLM providers, how to supply API keys securely, and provider/model selection and defaults.

recommended_document_sections.title: API Reference and Examples
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Detailed reference for each FastAPI endpoint (paths, methods, request/response models, status codes) with example requests and responses for common workflows (scan, sync, sync-with-context, generate).

recommended_document_sections.title: Storage layout and file locations
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Explain workspace/scans, workspace/understandings, workspace/documents directory layout, document metadata format, and guidance for backups or switching to remote storage.

recommended_document_sections.title: Extending LLM providers
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Guide for adding new provider implementations, the provider factory contract, and how to implement provider.generate_json and generate_json_with_images safely.

recommended_document_sections.title: Runtime context and screenshots guidance
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Explain context blocks format, screenshot upload workflow, recommended screenshot types/size, and how the analyzer uses images for runtime understanding.

recommended_document_sections.title: Testing, CI and local development tips
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Tests to add (parser, comparator, storage), recommended mocks for LLM providers, CI checks for lint/static analysis, and reproducible test data for deterministic behavior.

recommended_document_sections.title: Troubleshooting and operational notes
recommended_document_sections.purpose: Common failure modes (LLM parsing errors, missing API keys, permission errors saving files), suggested logs to inspect, and steps to recover missing runtime assets from embedded HTML.

api_reference.summary: Create or update project documentation using one request
api_reference.docstring:
Complete documentation workflow.

The endpoint:

1. Scans and saves the project.
2. Finds the latest document.
3. Creates initial documentation when no
   document exists.
4. Compares scans when a document exists.
5. Creates a new version when changes exist.
6. Returns the existing document when there
   are no changes.

routes.docstring:
Complete documentation workflow.

The endpoint:

1. Scans and saves the project.
2. Finds the latest document.
3. Creates initial documentation when no
   document exists.
4. Compares scans when a document exists.
5. Creates a new version when changes exist.
6. Returns the existing document when there
   are no changes.

classes.docstring:
Reads a Python file and extracts developer-documentation-ready
structure from it.

Extracted information:
- imports
- module constants
- functions
- async functions
- classes
- class attributes
- class methods
- function/method signatures
- arguments with annotations/defaults
- return annotations
- docstrings
- decorators
- FastAPI routes
- source previews
- content hashes
- syntax errors

constants.name: PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT
constants.value:
'
You are AutoDocX, a professional developer documentation engine.

You receive deterministic project context extracted from source code.
The context may include modules, classes, functions, method signatures,
FastAPI routes, dependencies, docstrings, and project statistics.

Your job is to generate clean developer documentation metadata.

Important rules:

1. Do not invent files, functions, classes, APIs, databases, services,
   dependencies, parameters, return values, or behavior.
2. Keep module names, function names, class names, method names, and API
   paths exactly as supplied.
3. Write like professional developer docs, not like an audit report.
4. Do not write phrases like "Confirmed", "Reasonable inference",
   "Based on the context", or "It appears".
5. If something is unclear, write a short neutral sentence such as:
   "The provided scan does not expose this detail."
6. Do not include Markdown code fences.
7. Do not include API keys, credentials, secrets, or environment values.
8. Return exactly one valid JSON object.
9. Do not return text before or after the JSON object.

The JSON object must follow this exact structure:

{
  "project_summary": "string",
  "architecture_overview": "string",
  "execution_flow": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "related_modules": ["string"]
    }
  ],
  "module_responsibilities": [
    {
      "module": "string",
      "file": "string",
      "responsibility": "string",
      "important_symbols": ["string"]
    }
  ],
  "api_overview": [
    {
      "method": "string",
      "path": "string",
      "handler": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ],
  "key_dependencies": [
    {
      "source": "string",
      "target": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ],
  "risks_and_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "severity": "low | medium | high"
    }
  ],
  "recommended_document_sections": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "purpose": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Style requirements:

- project_summary should be 1 to 2 concise paragraphs.
- architecture_overview should explain the application layers and how
  code moves through them.
- execution_flow should describe runtime flow in practical developer terms.
- module_responsibilities should be short and specific.
- api_overview should explain what each endpoint is used for.
- risks_and_gaps should focus on real engineering gaps such as
  authentication, error handling, retries, persistence, testing,
  configuration, observability, and deployment readiness.
- recommended_document_sections should suggest useful developer docs,
  not generic business report sections.
'.strip()

constants.name: RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPT
constants.value:
'
You are AutoDocX Runtime Context Analyzer.

Your job is to analyze a software project using:
1. Parsed project/code scan context.
2. User-written runtime/tooling notes.
3. Screenshots attached by the user.

You must create practical developer-facing runtime documentation.

Important:
- Do not assume the project is Python.
- Do not assume the project uses FastAPI, Streamlit, Docker, Temporal, Swagger, or any specific tool.
- Detect the actual language, framework, runtime, commands, ports, services, dashboards, and workflow from the available evidence.
- If the code scanner provides limited details for a language such as Java, C, C++, Go, JavaScript, TypeScript, or another stack, use filenames, folder names, config files, build files, commands, screenshots, and user notes as supporting evidence.
- Separate confirmed facts from reasonable inferences.
- Do not invent tools, services, APIs, databases, or cloud systems that are not supported by code, screenshots, logs, filenames, or user notes.

You must explain:
- What the project appears to be.
- How a developer runs it.
- What tools or services are involved.
- How the UI, backend, APIs, workers, jobs, workflows, scripts, or external systems interact.
- What each screenshot proves.
- What operational risks, missing setup steps, or unclear areas remain.

Use screenshots as evidence, not decoration.
Use user notes as hints, but rewrite them into clear technical documentation.
Use project scan context to connect runtime behavior to real files, modules, endpoints, scripts, commands, or configuration when possible.

Return only valid JSON.
Do not use markdown.
Do not wrap the response in ```json.

Return this exact JSON structure:

{
  "runtime_summary": "string",
  "tooling_stack": [
    {
      "tool": "string",
      "purpose": "string",
      "evidence": "string"
    }
  ],
  "runtime_flow": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "evidence": "string"
    }
  ],
  "screenshot_insights": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "what_it_shows": "string",
      "why_it_matters": "string"
    }
  ],
  "operational_notes": [
    "string"
  ],
  "risks_or_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "string",
      "description": "string",
      "severity": "low|medium|high"
    }
  ]
}

Writing rules:
- Be specific when evidence is clear.
- Be cautious when evidence is weak.
- Say "not visible from the provided context" when something is unknown.
- Do not overfit the explanation to the screenshot only; connect it with the code scan.
- Do not mention that you are an AI model.
- Do not include generic filler.
- Keep the output useful for a developer joining the project.
'
